proposal_text: This research proposes to develop an automated essay grading system for first-year undergraduate modules at Trinity College Dublin. The system will use large language model embeddings to score essays on a 0-100 scale by comparing them against a rubric-encoded scoring model trained on 3,000 previously graded essays. The system will provide both a numerical grade and written feedback comments generated by the LLM. The tool is intended to reduce marking workload by providing initial scores that lecturers can review and adjust. The training data consists of essays graded by twelve different tutors whose grading standards varied considerably. Students whose first language is not English may receive systematically different scores due to the model's sensitivity to grammatical patterns associated with non-native speakers. Students will not be informed that AI-assisted grading is being used. No appeals process specifically addressing algorithmic grading errors has been designed.

assessment.risk_summary: The proposal presents significant ethical risks due to potential discrimination against non-native English speakers, lack of transparency in AI grading, absence of an appeals process for algorithmic errors, and bias in training data from inconsistent tutor grading standards. The system's deployment could infringe on students' rights to fair assessment, privacy, and effective redress.

identified_risks.risk: Algorithmic bias against non-native English speakers due to model sensitivity to grammatical patterns
identified_risks.explanation: The system may systematically disadvantage non-English first language students, violating non-discrimination principles

identified_risks.risk: Lack of transparency in AI-assisted grading process
identified_risks.explanation: Students are not informed about AI involvement, undermining their right to understand grading mechanisms

identified_risks.risk: No specific appeals process for algorithmic grading errors
identified_risks.explanation: Absence of redress mechanisms violates rights to effective remedy and due process

identified_risks.risk: Training data variability from 12 tutors with inconsistent standards
identified_risks.explanation: Inconsistent training data may perpetuate grading biases and reduce system reliability

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Research must contribute to society and human well-being — discuss how applications may harm individuals...
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Conduct bias impact assessment and implement mitigation strategies

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Assess and communicate degree to which datasets are representative...
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Document dataset representativeness and demographic disparities in training data

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Assess whether organisation is obligated to conduct a FRIA...
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Perform mandatory Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA) for high-risk AI system

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: AI system must not autonomously make decisions... without human oversight...
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Implement mandatory lecturer review workflow for all AI-generated grades

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Possible negative social impacts on certain groups must be mitigated...
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Develop bias mitigation plan for non-native English speakers

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Consider whether your NLP/ML system could out, profile or endanger vulnerable individuals
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Evaluate risks to language minority students in grading outcomes

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: End-users must receive comprehensible information about AI logic...
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Provide clear disclosure about AI's role in grading to all students
Establish formal appeals process for disputing AI-generated grades

charter_rights_at_risk.relevance: System may systematically disadvantage non-native English speakers
Processing of student essays involves personal academic data
Use of 3,000 previously graded essays raises data minimization concerns
No specific appeals process for algorithmic grading errors

historical_precedents.lesson: Unaddressed training data biases can lead to systematic discrimination

historical_precedents.incident_title: Google Photos Labeled Black People as Gorillas
historical_precedents.lesson: Algorithmic outputs can cause profound social harm through dehumanization
Lack of transparency in AI systems undermines trust and due process
AI systems can produce harmful outputs when safety measures are insufficient

recommended_mitigations.mitigation: Conduct mandatory FRIA and publish results publicly
Implement opt-out mechanism for AI-assisted grading
Develop bias audit protocol for non-native English speakers
Create standardized appeals process for grading disputes
Add explainability features for AI grading decisions

tier1_mandatory_actions: Implement lecturer override capability for all AI-generated grades
Publish detailed FRIA report with mitigation strategies
Establish formal appeals process for algorithmic grading errors
Add opt-out option for students uncomfortable with AI-assisted grading
tier2_reflective_prompts: How might the system's feedback comments inadvertently stigmatize non-native writing styles?
What long-term impacts could arise from normalizing AI grading in educational contexts?
Should the system be modified to provide alternative grading perspectives when there's high model uncertainty?
